treatment.warning_message: Take quick action within 48 hours to prevent further crop losses.

treatments.0: Apply fungicides at first signs of infection

advice.0: Plant early to avoid peak rust season

symptoms.0: The first stage appears as small pale yellow spots.
symptoms.1: Followed by elongated reddish-brown pustules on leaf sheaths and stems.
symptoms.2: The second stage shows black shiny pustules on stems and spike sheaths.
symptoms.3: Infected plants are short, prone to lodging, and may die in severe cases.

treatments.0: Preventive spraying before severe spread using systemic fungicides.
treatments.2: Use fine spray coverage to reach the stem.
treatments.3: Remove alternate host plants if present.

advice.0: Rotate crops for 2-3 years with non-host crops, avoid previous wheat residues.
advice.1: Plant resistant varieties such as Morocco or Little Club.
advice.2: Balanced fertilization and reduce excessive nitrogen.
advice.3: Regular weather monitoring, plant slightly late to avoid spring infection.

symptoms.0: Aphids cluster on leaves and stems causing curling and yellowing.
symptoms.1: Produce honeydew that attracts ants and black mold.
symptoms.2: Heavy infestations reduce growth and yield.

treatments.0: Spray systemic insecticides when economic threshold is reached.
treatments.1: Repeat treatment after 7-10 days if necessary.

medicines.0: Consult a local agricultural specialist for registered products.

advice.0: Encourage natural predators such as ladybugs.
advice.1: Regularly monitor fields using yellow sticky traps.

symptoms.0: Initial symptoms on leaves appear as water-soaked gray or oval spots.
symptoms.1: They become gray or brown with dark edges or a yellow halo.
symptoms.2: On spikes, partial or full bleaching appears starting from a gray-black infection point.
symptoms.3: Spikes become white, shriveled, and empty.
symptoms.4: These symptoms usually appear during flowering in warm, humid conditions.

treatments.0: Main treatment relies on systemic fungicides.
treatments.1: Spray twice with a 7-10 day interval.
treatments.2: Effectiveness is reduced in high humidity conditions.
treatments.3: Seed treatment before planting helps reduce spread.

advice.0: Plant resistant varieties and adjust sowing date.
advice.1: Use certified treated seeds, avoid dense planting and excessive nitrogen.
advice.2: Proper crop rotation and weather monitoring for preventive spraying.

symptoms.0: The disease starts with small orange or light brown spots on the lower leaf surface.
symptoms.1: These spots gradually expand into orange powdery pustules.
symptoms.2: As the disease progresses, pustules turn dark brown and the epidermis may rupture.
symptoms.3: Severe infections cause leaf drying, yellowing, and smaller spikes.

treatments.0: Immediate spraying with systemic fungicides at first symptoms.
treatments.1: Repeat spraying after 10-14 days if high humidity persists.
treatments.2: Spray in early morning or evening with sufficient water volume.
treatments.3: Remove and destroy infected plants early in small fields.

advice.2: Weekly monitoring especially at 20-25°C and humidity >80%.
advice.3: Rotate with legumes and dispose of crop residues.

symptoms.0: Symptoms begin at the seedling stage with wilting and browning of the stem base.
symptoms.1: Plants pull out easily and are pale yellow, with shriveled spikes.
symptoms.2: Results in stunting, weak spikes, and reduced yield.

treatments.0: Seed treatment before planting with systemic fungicides.
treatments.1: Apply soil drench or root irrigation in early stages if symptoms appear.
treatments.2: Improve drainage and remove infected plants in small fields.
treatments.3: Use Bacillus subtilis as a biological option.

advice.0: Rotate crops for 2-3 years with non-host crops such as legumes.
advice.1: Use certified pre-treated seeds and balanced fertilization rich in phosphorus and potassium.
advice.2: Improve aeration and avoid excessive moisture.

symptoms.0: Seedling blight may occur, but the most visible symptom is spike bleaching.
symptoms.1: Spikes turn pinkish-light brown in warm, humid weather with visible fungal growth.
symptoms.2: Grains are small, shriveled, and may be contaminated with dangerous DON toxins.

treatments.0: Immediate spraying with systemic fungicides at the start of flowering.
treatments.1: Repeat spraying after 3-5 days under high humidity.
treatments.2: Seed treatment before planting and reduce prolonged moisture.

advice.0: Plant resistant varieties such as Sumai 3 or Ernie.
advice.1: Avoid late irrigation or excessive nitrogen.
advice.2: Monitor weather during flowering and use clean seeds.
advice.3: Split irrigation to avoid prolonged moisture.

symptoms.0: Symptoms begin as small dark brown to black spots on leaves and bases.
symptoms.1: They expand into oval or elongated spots that merge on the leaf blade.
symptoms.2: Results in short plants and small or empty grains.

treatments.0: Immediate spraying with systemic fungicides at first spots.
treatments.1: Repeat every 10-14 days under high humidity.
treatments.2: Use sufficient water volume with adjuvants to improve adhesion.
treatments.3: Remove infected leaves in small fields and improve ventilation.

advice.0: Use resistant varieties and pre-treated seeds.
advice.2: Avoid excessive nitrogen and increase phosphorus and potassium.
advice.3: Moderate irrigation with good ventilation and weekly spring monitoring.

symptoms.0: Small yellow spots appear on lower leaves in early growth stages.
symptoms.1: A white powdery layer develops on leaves, stems, and spikes.
symptoms.2: The coating turns brown or gray, causing leaf drying.

treatments.0: Apply preventive or curative fungicides at first signs of infection.
treatments.1: Repeat treatment every 7-10 days in humid conditions.
treatments.2: Neem oil or sodium bicarbonate solutions can be used in small fields.

medicines.0: Consult local agricultural recommendations for registered fungicides.

advice.0: Avoid dense planting and excessive nitrogen fertilization.
advice.1: Rotate crops and remove volunteer wheat.
advice.2: Use treated seeds and regularly monitor humidity levels.

symptoms.0: Mites are invisible to the naked eye, but symptoms include pale, curled leaf tips.
symptoms.1: Leaves may curl into a tube shape and plants may be stunted.

treatments.0: Treatment is difficult, but miticides can be sprayed when economic threshold is exceeded.
treatments.1: Repeat every 7-10 days with fine spray.
treatments.2: Destroy volunteer wheat and use mineral oils when needed.

advice.0: Destroy volunteer wheat and weeds before planting.
advice.1: Delay planting slightly when needed and avoid adjacent host crops.
advice.2: Release natural predators such as ladybugs if possible.

symptoms.0: Symptoms start on lower leaves as small water-soaked yellow spots.
symptoms.1: They expand into longitudinal or oval spots with a wide yellow halo.
symptoms.2: Spots merge causing leaf death and shedding.

treatments.0: Preventive-curative spraying starting from late winter or at first spots.
treatments.1: Repeat 2-3 times every 10-14 days under high humidity.
treatments.2: Use 300-400 L/ha with adjuvants to reach lower leaves.

advice.0: Three-year rotation with legumes or brassicas.
advice.1: Moderate irrigation and avoid prolonged moisture.
advice.2: Weekly spring monitoring at 15-25°C and high humidity.
advice.3: Avoid dense planting and excessive nitrogen.

symptoms.0: Masses of powdery black spores replacing grain kernels.
symptoms.1: Infected plants may show stunted growth.
symptoms.2: Soot-like black layer on spikes and seeds.

treatments.1: Apply fungicide seed treatments before planting.
treatments.2: Remove and destroy infected plant material.

medicines.0: Consult a local agricultural specialist for registered fungicides.

advice.0: Rotate crops to break the disease cycle.
advice.1: Avoid planting in fields with a history of smut.
advice.2: Regularly monitor fields and remove infected plants early.

symptoms.0: Young leaves turn yellow and wilt (dead heart symptoms).
symptoms.1: Larvae tunnel inside the stem causing internal damage.

treatments.0: Apply insecticides during early tillering stage.
treatments.1: Use systemic insecticides for effective control.
treatments.2: Remove and destroy damaged tillers immediately.

medicines.0: Consult a local agricultural specialist for registered insecticides.

advice.0: Early sowing to avoid peak fly population periods.
advice.1: Avoid late sowing which increases susceptibility.
advice.2: Regularly monitor crops for early detection.

symptoms.0: Tan to brown oval lesions with a yellow halo on leaves.
symptoms.1: Lesions may coalesce causing large dead areas on leaves.
symptoms.2: Severe infections reduce photosynthesis and yield.

treatments.0: Apply foliar fungicides at first signs of infection.
treatments.2: Remove crop residues after harvest to reduce infection source.

medicines.0: Consult a local agricultural specialist for registered fungicides.

advice.0: Rotate crops to reduce soil infection sources.
advice.1: Avoid excessive irrigation that promotes leaf wetness.
advice.2: Monitor fields regularly especially in humid conditions.

alerts.title: Disease Alerts
alerts.subtitle: Based on weather forecasts for the next 7 days
alerts.no_risks_body: Next week's weather conditions are favorable for crops.

yellow_rust.name: Yellow Rust
yellow_rust.trigger_crit_day: At least one day with high humidity and ideal infection temperature
yellow_rust.trigger_opt_day: Several days with suitable conditions for spore germination
yellow_rust.trigger_temp_optimal: Average temperature 10–15°C: ideal for yellow rust spread
yellow_rust.trigger_temp_acceptable: Average temperature 15–20°C: suitable for yellow rust development
yellow_rust.trigger_temp_inhibit: Temperature above 21°C: limits yellow rust development
yellow_rust.prevention_0: Apply preventive fungicide (propiconazole or tebuconazole) before infection
yellow_rust.prevention_1: Use resistant or tolerant wheat varieties
yellow_rust.prevention_2: Monitor fields weekly and record early symptoms
yellow_rust.prevention_3: Remove and dispose of infected crop residues after harvest
yellow_rust.prevention_4: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes soft leaf tissue

brown_rust.name: Brown Rust
brown_rust.trigger_infection_days_high: 4+ days with humidity >60% and temperature 8–16°C: high infection risk
brown_rust.trigger_infection_days_low: 2–3 days with humidity >60% and temperature 8–16°C: moderate risk
brown_rust.trigger_high_hum: 3+ days with humidity >80% and temperature 8–25°C: very favorable for spread
brown_rust.trigger_temp_optimal: Average temperature 15–25°C: ideal for brown rust development
brown_rust.trigger_temp_acceptable: Average temperature 8–15°C: suitable for brown rust development
brown_rust.prevention_0: Apply fungicide at first signs of orange pustules on leaves
brown_rust.prevention_2: Avoid dense planting to improve airflow
brown_rust.prevention_3: Remove infected residues and rotate with legumes

stem_rust.name: Stem Rust
stem_rust.trigger_hot_humid_high: 3+ days at 18–30°C and humidity >60%: critical conditions
stem_rust.trigger_hot_humid_low: At least one day with hot, humid conditions favorable for stem rust
stem_rust.trigger_night_opt: 3+ nights at 15–22°C and humidity >55%: ideal for spore germination
stem_rust.prevention_0: Apply triazole or strobilurin fungicide at first signs on stems
stem_rust.prevention_1: Use Ug99-resistant varieties if available in your region
stem_rust.prevention_2: Avoid late planting that exposes crops to warm spring conditions
stem_rust.prevention_3: Monitor and remove Berberis (barberry), the alternate host

septoria.name: Septoria Leaf Blotch
septoria.trigger_epic_high: 3+ days with humidity >85% and temperature 15–25°C: epidemic conditions
septoria.trigger_epic_low: At least one day with very high humidity and optimal temperature
septoria.trigger_very_hum_high: 4+ days with humidity ≥90%: very high risk of Septoria spread
septoria.trigger_temp_optimal: Average temperature 15–20°C: ideal for Septoria tritici
septoria.trigger_temp_acceptable: Average temperature 20–25°C: still suitable for Septoria
septoria.trigger_temp_stop: Average temperature below 4°C: disease development stops
septoria.prevention_0: Apply propiconazole or tebuconazole at GS31–37 (stem elongation)
septoria.prevention_1: Use partially resistant varieties and rotate with non-cereal crops
septoria.prevention_2: Avoid dense planting and excessive nitrogen fertilization
septoria.prevention_3: Remove crop residues to reduce infection source
septoria.prevention_4: Apply a second fungicide treatment after 14 days if humidity remains high

fusarium.name: Fusarium Head Blight
fusarium.trigger_rapid: 2+ days with humidity >95% and temperature 16–30°C: rapid spread risk
fusarium.trigger_crit_high: 2+ days with humidity >90% and temperature 15–30°C: critical risk
fusarium.trigger_crit_low: One day with humidity >90% and temperature 15–30°C: notable risk
fusarium.trigger_temp_optimal: Average temperature 25–30°C: ideal for Fusarium and mycotoxin production
fusarium.trigger_temp_acceptable: Average temperature 20–25°C: suitable for Fusarium development
fusarium.prevention_0: Apply tebuconazole or miconazole at 50–75% of flowering stage
fusarium.prevention_1: Use Fusarium-resistant or tolerant wheat varieties
fusarium.prevention_2: Avoid corn residues in the field (primary infection source)
fusarium.prevention_3: Do not over-apply nitrogen, especially during flowering
fusarium.prevention_4: Test harvested grain for DON mycotoxins if risk was high

mildew.name: Powdery Mildew
mildew.trigger_sweet_high: 4+ days with humidity ≥85% and temperature 12–22°C: ideal conditions for mildew
mildew.trigger_sweet_low: 2–3 days with high humidity and ideal temperature for mildew
mildew.trigger_temp_optimal: Average temperature 15–21°C: ideal for mildew development
mildew.trigger_temp_acceptable: Average temperature 12–15°C: suitable for mildew development
mildew.trigger_dew: 3+ days with large temperature variation (≥12°C): promotes dew formation
mildew.trigger_mod_hum: 3+ days with moderate humidity (70–85%) and ideal temperature
mildew.trigger_diurnal: Diurnal temperature variation ≥10°C: promotes dew deposition on leaves
mildew.prevention_0: Apply triadimenol, tebuconazole, or myclobutanil at first white spots
mildew.prevention_1: Use mildew-resistant or tolerant wheat varieties
mildew.prevention_2: Reduce planting density to improve airflow
mildew.prevention_3: Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization which promotes soft growth
mildew.prevention_4: Monitor lower leaves from the tillering stage onwards

tan_spot.name: Tan Spot
tan_spot.trigger_opt_high: 3+ days with humidity >80% and temperature 18–28°C: optimal conditions
tan_spot.trigger_opt_low: At least one day with humidity >80% and temperature 18–28°C
tan_spot.trigger_fast_high: 3+ days with humidity >75% and temperature 20–27°C: fast disease cycle
tan_spot.trigger_fast_low: Two days with humidity >75% and temperature 20–27°C
tan_spot.trigger_min_days: 5+ days with humidity >70% and temperature 10–30°C: persistent risk
tan_spot.trigger_temp_optimal: Average temperature 20–27°C: ideal for tan spot lesion formation
tan_spot.prevention_0: Apply propiconazole or tebuconazole at first brown lesions with dark edges
tan_spot.prevention_1: Till or remove wheat straw (primary source of Pyrenophora tritici-repentis)
tan_spot.prevention_2: Rotate with non-host crops (canola, legumes) for 2+ years

root_rot.name: Root Rot
root_rot.trigger_waterlog_high: 3+ days with humidity >90% and temperature below 20°C: waterlogging risk
root_rot.trigger_waterlog_low: At least one day with very high humidity and moderate temperature
root_rot.trigger_hum_high: 4+ days with humidity >85%: persistent soil saturation conditions
root_rot.prevention_0: Improve field drainage (ditches, drains, raised beds)
root_rot.prevention_1: Use thiram + carboxin or tebuconazole as seed treatment before planting
root_rot.prevention_2: Rotate with legumes (chickpeas, lentils) for 2–3 years
root_rot.prevention_3: Avoid waterlogged or compacted soil; deep tillage when needed

messages.temp_high: Temperature is high ({value}°C); early morning irrigation recommended
messages.high_humidity: High humidity detected; reduce irrigation frequency to limit disease spread
messages.low_humidity: Low humidity; slightly increase irrigation frequency to avoid plant stress
messages.high_wind: High wind speed; ensure sufficient soil moisture to prevent drying
messages.high_water_demand: ⚠️ High water demand detected; monitor soil moisture carefully

timing.morning_dry: Early morning (5–7 AM) to allow fast leaf drying
timing.root_morning: Morning only with low-volume controlled irrigation
timing.evening_mite: Late evening while maintaining moderate soil moisture
timing.default: Early morning or late evening with ground-level irrigation

rust.rationale: Rust diseases spread faster in humid conditions; reduce irrigation slightly to minimize leaf wetness.

septoria.rationale: Septoria thrives in wet conditions; significantly reduce irrigation to limit disease pressure.

mildew.rationale: Powdery mildew is less affected by irrigation volume; maintain regular irrigation with good ventilation.

tan_spot.rationale: Tan spot spreads in persistent humid conditions; moderate reduction in irrigation is recommended.

blast.rationale: Blast develops rapidly in wet environments; reduce irrigation frequency.

fusarium.rationale: Fusarium head blight favors wet conditions; reduce excessive irrigation.

leaf_blight.rationale: Leaf blight intensity increases with higher leaf wetness; moderate irrigation reduction is recommended.

root_rot.rationale: Root rot is strongly linked to overwatering and poor drainage; significantly reduce irrigation.

smut.rationale: Smut is weakly related to irrigation; a slight reduction is applied as a precaution.

aphid.rationale: Aphid outbreaks may stress the plant; maintain slightly higher moisture to aid recovery.

mite.rationale: Spider mites thrive in dry conditions; slightly increase irrigation and humidity near the soil.

stem_fly.rationale: Stem fly damage is not strongly affected by irrigation levels.

healthy.rationale: Plant is healthy; standard irrigation practices are applied.

unknown.rationale: Unknown condition detected; a conservative irrigation adjustment is applied.
recommendations: Confirm diagnosis with an agricultural expert
